article_title: Cut AI token usage by 96%? Here's how AWS Strands Agents does it.
core_thesis: The article argues that designing intent-based tools and using semantic search to limit the tool catalog presented to an AI agent can dramatically reduce token consumption (by up to 96%) while improving accuracy and reasoning. AWS developer advocate Morgan Willis demonstrates this through a Strands Agents demo, showing that narrowly scoped agents with fewer, outcome-aligned tools outperform general-purpose agents that expose many raw API endpoints.
detailed_summary: The article covers a podcast episode where AWS developer advocate Morgan Willis explains and demonstrates the Strands Agents framework, an open-source agentic framework with over 14 million downloads. The core demo involves an accounting API that looks up the latest invoice for a customer. In the first iteration, each API endpoint is mapped directly to an agent tool, resulting in five chained API calls and approximately 52,000 tokens consumed. In the second iteration, Willis replaces these with intent-based tools—tools designed around an outcome (e.g., 'get latest invoice') rather than a data operation (e.g., 'get customer', 'get invoice list'). This reduces the task to a single tool call and only 2,000 tokens, a 96% reduction. The third iteration moves tools to a remote MCP server via AWS Agent Core Gateway and enables semantic search across the tool catalog, so the agent only receives the tools relevant to each query (instead of all 16), cutting token usage roughly in half again. Willis emphasizes that narrowly scoped agents tend to perform better than general-purpose ones, and that as MCP servers proliferate, controlling which tools an agent sees on each run becomes critical.

entities.name: Morgan Willis
entities.role: AWS developer advocate who demonstrates Strands Agents and explains the token reduction techniques.

entities.name: AWS Strands Agents
entities.role: Open-source agentic framework by AWS, used to build and run AI agents with efficient tool design.

entities.name: AWS Agent Core Gateway
entities.role: AWS service that enables remote MCP server integration and semantic tool search for agents.

entities.name: Model Context Protocol (MCP)
entities.role: Protocol used to expose tools to agents; the article discusses MCP servers and tool catalogs.

key_data_points: The first iteration of the demo (direct API endpoint mapping) consumed ~52,000 tokens for a single invoice lookup task. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: understanding token consumption helps in resource-exhaustion attacks or cost-based denial of service.)

Switching to intent-based tools reduced token usage to ~2,000 tokens—a 96% reduction—by rolling multiple API calls into one outcome-aligned tool. (🟢 Relevant to programming: designing intent-based tools is a best practice for efficient agent development.)

Enabling semantic search via AWS Agent Core Gateway further halved token usage compared to loading all tools, because only relevant tools are presented to the agent. (🟢 Relevant to DevOps/homelab: semantic search over tool catalogs can be implemented in custom agent systems.)

Willis states: 'The fewer tools that you expose to your agent, the less likely it is to call the wrong one.' This principle improves both accuracy and cost efficiency. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: limiting tool exposure reduces attack surface for tool misuse.)

Narrowly scoped agents outperform general-purpose agents in context efficiency, speed, and accuracy. (🟢 Relevant to student: this design principle applies to any AI project, including study assistants.)
methodology_steps: Step 1: Map each API endpoint directly to an agent tool (naive approach). This results in many tools and high token usage due to chained calls.

Step 2: Refactor tools to be intent-based—design each tool around a business outcome (e.g., 'get latest invoice') rather than a data operation. Combine multiple API calls into a single tool to reduce the number of tools and calls.

Step 3: Host tools on a remote MCP server and use a gateway (e.g., AWS Agent Core Gateway) with semantic search. This ensures the agent only receives tools relevant to the current query, further reducing token consumption.
Step 4: Narrowly scope agents to specific tasks rather than building a general-purpose agent. This allows precise tool design and better performance.
conclusions: Intent-based tools can reduce token usage by up to 96% compared to exposing raw API endpoints, because they align with the agent's reasoning and reduce the number of calls. This is a key design pattern for cost-effective AI agents.

Semantic search over a tool catalog is an effective way to limit the tools an agent sees per query, improving both efficiency and accuracy. As MCP servers grow, tool selection becomes as important as tool design.

Narrowly scoped agents outperform general-purpose agents. Developers should design agents for specific tasks and expose only the minimal set of tools needed, which also reduces the risk of incorrect tool selection.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your interests in AI engineering and programming. As a programmer building AI agents or integrating LLMs, the technique of intent-based tool design can drastically reduce API costs and improve agent reliability. For your homelab/DevOps projects, you can apply the same principle by designing custom tools that encapsulate multiple operations, and use semantic search to dynamically select tools. From a Red Teaming perspective, understanding how tool exposure affects agent behavior can help you identify attack surfaces—e.g., if an agent has too many tools, it may be more prone to misuse or prompt injection. The article also reinforces the value of narrow agent scoping, a principle you can apply to any automation project.
extra_advice: Consider implementing a lightweight MCP server in your homelab that exposes intent-based tools for common tasks (e.g., system monitoring, network diagnostics). Use a simple embedding-based semantic search (e.g., with sentence-transformers) to dynamically select tools per query. This will give you hands-on experience with the patterns described and can be extended for Red Teaming experiments, such as testing how tool selection affects agent behavior under adversarial prompts.
